ok , i want you to make a web app with this 3d model , allow me to download the revelant files so that i can display it on my landing page too , but create the following 3d model ; Create a parametric humanoid robot manipulator: a 7-DOF serial robot arm with an
anthropomorphic 21-DOF five-finger hand mounted on its tool flange. Units: mm.
Coordinate frame: Z-up, arm base at origin, arm posed straight up (all joints at
0°). Style: smooth industrial collaborative-robot look (like a Franka/KUKA iiwa),
white body shells with dark grey joint rings. Expose all key dimensions as
parameters at the top of the script.

Since this is a static CAD model, represent every degree of freedom as visible
joint geometry: roll joints as cylindrical collars with a 1.5 mm seam groove,
pitch joints as pancake housings with a clevis fork + pin bosses, and 2-DOF
finger knuckles as two stacked orthogonal pin hinges (gimbal).

=== ARM (7 DOF, S-R-S kinematic chain, bottom to top) ===

BASE: cylindrical pedestal, dia 160, height 110, 6 mm top fillet, four M8
clearance holes on a 130 mm bolt circle in a 12 mm thick base flange.

J1 (yaw, axis = Z): joint collar dia 110, height 80, sitting on the base, with
a horizontal seam groove showing the rotation plane.

J2 (shoulder pitch, axis = Y, horizontal): pancake joint housing dia 110,
width 90, oriented with its cylinder axis horizontal, blended into the top of
the J1 collar with a rounded shoulder block.

LINK 1 (upper arm): tapered cylinder from dia 100 to dia 85, length 320,
running vertically (+Z) from the shoulder.

J3 (upper-arm roll, axis along the link): inline collar dia 85, height 60,
integrated mid-link with a seam groove.

J4 (elbow pitch, axis = Y): pancake housing dia 85, width 75, with a
characteristic 70 mm lateral elbow offset (the link above is shifted 70 mm in
+X relative to the link below, connected by a smooth S-curve hull).

LINK 2 (forearm): tapered cylinder from dia 75 to dia 65, length 280, vertical.

J5 (forearm roll, axis along the link): inline collar dia 65, height 50,
with seam groove.

J6 (wrist pitch, axis = Y): pancake housing dia 60, width 55.

J7 (tool roll, axis = Z): collar dia 55, height 45, ending in an ISO 9409-1
style tool flange: dia 63, thickness 10, four M6 holes on a 50 mm bolt circle,
31.5 mm center pilot recess.

=== HAND (21 DOF, mounted on the tool flange, fingers pointing up) ===

PALM: rounded box 95 long x 85 wide x 28 thick, 8 mm edge fillets, with a
cylindrical wrist boss (dia 50, height 20) joining it to the tool flange. Palm
plane faces +X (palmar side flat, dorsal side slightly domed).

FOUR FINGERS (index, middle, ring, little), each with 4 DOF, mounted along the
top edge of the palm, spaced 22 mm apart, splayed outward 0/3/6/9 degrees:

- MCP knuckle, 2 DOF: a gimbal of two stacked orthogonal pin hinges, each a
  dia 16 cylinder with visible pin end-caps — first hinge axis perpendicular
  to the palm plane (abduction/adduction), second axis parallel to the palm
  plane (flexion).
- Proximal phalanx: capsule-shaped link dia 18, length 48, flat palmar face.
- PIP joint, 1 DOF: clevis fork + tongue pin hinge, pin dia 12.
- Middle phalanx: capsule dia 15, length 28, flat palmar face.
- DIP joint, 1 DOF: clevis pin hinge, pin dia 10.
- Distal phalanx: dia 13, length 24, hemispherical fingertip with a flat
  elliptical fingertip pad on the palmar side.

Scale whole fingers: middle = 1.0, index = 0.95, ring = 0.95, little = 0.80.

THUMB, 5 DOF, mounted on the palmar-radial corner of the palm on a 45-degree
angled base boss:

- CMC joint, 2 DOF: gimbal of two stacked orthogonal pin hinges, dia 18.
- Thumb metacarpal: capsule dia 19, length 45.
- Axial opposition joint, 1 DOF: inline roll collar dia 17 with seam groove,
  in the middle of the metacarpal.
- MCP joint, 1 DOF: clevis pin hinge dia 14.
- Proximal phalanx: capsule dia 16, length 35.
- IP joint, 1 DOF: clevis pin hinge dia 11.
- Distal phalanx: dia 14, length 28, hemispherical tip with flat pad.

DOF checksum: arm = 7 (J1–J7); hand = 4 fingers x 4 + thumb 5 = 21; total 28
visible joints. Every joint must be physically represented in the geometry.

Parameters to expose: base_dia, link1_len, link2_len, elbow_offset, joint
diameters per joint, palm dimensions, finger_spacing, finger scale factors,
phalanx lengths/diameters, seam_groove_width, pin clearance (0.4 default).

I'll build a self-contained web app: a parametric Three.js model builder (`robot.js`) that constructs the full 7-DOF arm + 21-DOF hand procedurally in mm with all dimensions exposed as parameters, plus a viewer (`index.html`) with orbit controls, a live parameter GUI, and download buttons (GLB export for your landing page + the source module). Then I'll smoke-test it headlessly and screenshot it to verify the geometry actually looks right.

Let me start with the parametric model module — it's the core artifact:

blocks.input_json:
{"content":"# Parametric Humanoid Robot Manipulator — Web Viewer

A 7-DOF collaborative robot arm with an anthropomorphic **21-DOF five-finger hand**
on its tool flange, built procedurally in three.js. Units are **mm**, frame is
**Z-up**, posed straight up (all joints at 0°). White shells, dark grey joint
rings — Franka / KUKA iiwa style.

Every degree of freedom is visible geometry:

| Joint type | Representation |
|---|---|
| Roll joints (J1, J3, J5, J7, thumb opposition) | cylindrical collar with 1.5 mm seam groove |
| Pitch joints (J2, J4, J6) | pancake housing + clevis fork + pin bosses |
| 1-DOF finger joints (PIP, DIP, thumb MCP/IP) | clevis fork + tongue pin hinge |
| 2-DOF knuckles (finger MCP, thumb CMC) | gimbal of two stacked orthogonal pin hinges |

**DOF checksum:** arm 7 (J1–J7) + hand 21 (4 fingers × 4 + thumb 5) = **28**.
The viewer counts the joints in the built model and shows the total in the HUD.

## Files

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `robot.js` | The parametric model builder (the part you reuse). Exports `buildRobot(params)`, `defaultParams`, `countDOF`, `disposeRobot`. |
| `index.html` | Full viewer: orbit controls, live parameter GUI, **Download .glb** / **robot.js** buttons. |
| `embed.html` | Minimal copy-paste example for a landing page (no GUI, turntable, ~60 lines). |
| `test.mjs` | Node smoke test (DOF checksum, triangle count, bounding box). |

## Run it

Needs a static server (ES modules don't load from `file://`):

```bash
cd robot-arm-viewer
python3 -m http.server 8123
# open http://localhost:8123
```

`index.html?view=hand` starts with the camera framed on the hand.

## Put it on your landing page

**Option A — live parametric model (recommended).**
Copy `robot.js` into your site and use the snippet in `embed.html`. It needs no
build step — just an import map pointing at the three.js CDN, a sized `<div>`,
and ~50 lines of viewer code. You keep full parametric control
(`buildRobot({ elbow_offset: 90 })`) and the payload is tiny (one JS file).

**Option B — static .glb + `<model-viewer>` (simplest).**
Click **Download .glb** in the viewer (the export is converted to glTF
conventions: Y-up, meters), then:

```html
<script type="module"
  src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@google/model-viewer@3.5.0/dist/model-viewer.min.js"></script>

<model-viewer src="«HIGH_ENTROPY».glb"
  camera-controls auto-rotate shadow-intensity="1"
  style="width:100%;height:560px"></model-viewer>
```

The `.glb` also drops straight into Blender, Unity, Spline, Framer, Webflow, etc.

## Parameters (excerpt)

All dimensions live in `defaultParams` at the top of `robot.js`; pass overrides
to `buildRobot({...})`. Key ones:

| Parameter | Default | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `base_dia` / `base_height` | 160 / 110 | pedestal, M8×4 on 130 mm bolt circle in 12 mm flange |
| `j1_dia` … `j7_dia` | 110…55 | per-joint diameters (collars and pancake housings) |
| `link1_len` / `link2_len` | 320 / 280 | upper arm / forearm lengths (tapered 100→85, 75→65) |
| `elbow_offset` | 70 | lateral +X offset above J4, bridged by an S-curve hull |
| `flange_dia` | 63 | ISO 9409-1 tool flange, M6×4 on 50 mm BC, 31.5 mm pilot |
| `palm_len/w/t` | 95 / 85 / 28 | palm rounded box, 8 mm fillets, palmar side +X |
| `finger_spacing` | 22 | finger pitch along palm top edge |
| `finger_splay` | [0,3,6,9]° | outward fan per finger |
| `finger_scales` | 0.95 / 1.0 / 0.95 / 0.80 | index / middle / ring / little |
| `prox/mid/dist_len` | 48 / 28 / 24 | phalanx lengths (dia 18 / 15 / 13) |
| `thumb_*` | — | metacarpal 45×ø19, opposition collar ø17, tilt/splay/twist pose |
| `seam_groove_width` | 1.5 | roll-joint seam grooves |
| `pin_clearance` | 0.4 | shown as dark clearance rings around hinge pins |

Done — the web app is built, tested, and running at **http://localhost:8123** (server is live in the background). Everything is in `~/robot-arm-viewer/`.

## What you got

**The model** — built procedurally in three.js (mm, Z-up, all joints at 0°), white shells with dark grey joint rings. All 28 DOF are physically visible: roll joints as seam-grooved collars (J1/J3/J5/J7 + thumb opposition), pitch joints as pancake housings with clevis forks and pin bosses (J2/J4/J6), finger knuckles as stacked orthogonal pin-hinge gimbals, plus the 70 mm elbow offset with S-curve hull, ISO 9409-1 tool flange, bolted base flange, splayed/scaled fingers, and the 45°-mounted opposed thumb. The smoke test (`node test.mjs`) verifies the DOF checksum: 7 arm + 16 finger + 5 thumb = **28 named joints**, ~88k triangles, no degenerate geometry. I verified it visually with headless-Chrome screenshots of both the full robot and a hand close-up.

## Files for your landing page

| File | Use |
|---|---|
| `robot-arm-hand.min.glb` | **140 KB Draco-compressed model** — the easiest path: drop into any page with `<model-viewer>` (snippet in README). Render-verified after compression. |
| `robot.js` | The parametric builder — every dimension from the spec is in `defaultParams` at the top (`buildRobot({ elbow_offset: 90 })` etc.) |
| `embed.html` | Copy-paste live three.js embed (~50 lines, turntable, no build step) |
| `index.html` | Full viewer: orbit controls, live parameter GUI (link lengths, elbow offset, finger spacing/scales, thumb pose…), **Download .glb** / **robot.js** buttons |
| `bake_glb.mjs` | Regenerates the GLB from the params (`node bake_glb.mjs`, then re-run the gltf-transform command in the README to re-compress) |
| `robot-arm-hand.glb` | Uncompressed 5 MB export for Blender/Unity/Spline |

The GLB exports are converted to glTF conventions (Y-up, meters) so they behave correctly in `<model-viewer>`, Framer, Webflow, Blender, etc. Tip: `index.html?view=hand` opens with the camera framed on the hand so you can inspect the 21-DOF finger detail up close.

{"content":"# ROBIOS — Parametric Robot Manipulator with Pipetting Animation

A 7-DOF collaborative robot arm with an anthropomorphic **21-DOF five-finger
hand**, black body shells with white **ROBIOS** engraving (base front/back,
upper arm, forearm, palm), built procedurally in three.js as a true kinematic
hierarchy. Units are **mm**, frame is **Z-up**.

The robot grips a micropipette and runs a looping **pipetting cycle**
(13 s, seamless): reach → hover over the beaker → dip → aspirate (liquid rises
into the tip, beaker level drops, plunger releases) → swing to the 24-well
plate → dispense into two wells (droplets fall, wells fill) → return home.
Arm poses are solved with closed-form IK (verified < 0.01 mm against the scene
graph FK) and baked into a standard glTF **AnimationClip** named `pipetting`,
so the animation travels with the model everywhere.

**DOF checksum:** arm 7 (J1–J7) + hand 21 (4 fingers × 4 + thumb 5) = **28**
named joint pivots (`robot.userData.joints`).

## Files

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `robios-pipetting.min.glb` | **Animated scene (2.9 MB, Draco)** — robot + beaker + well plate + liquids + `pipetting` clip. Drop into any landing page. |
| `robios-arm.min.glb` | Static robot only (178 KB, Draco), open hand, no props. |
| `robot.js` | Parametric articulated model builder. `buildRobot(params)`, `defaultParams`, `setBrandFont`, `countDOF`, `disposeRobot`. |
| `anim.js` | Lab props + pipette + IK + clip baker. `setupPipettingStage`, `solveArmIK`, `buildPipettingClip`, `LAB` layout. |
| `index.html` | Full viewer: animation playback, parameter GUI, all download buttons. |
| `embed.html` | Copy-paste live three.js embed for a landing page (~70 lines). |
| `bake_glb.mjs` | Regenerates both `.glb` files (`node bake_glb.mjs`). |
| `test.mjs` | Smoke test: DOF checksum, IK-vs-FK accuracy, clip integrity. |
| `robios-pipetting.glb` / `robios-arm.glb` | Uncompressed exports for «HIGH_ENTROPY». |

## Run it

```bash
cd robot-arm-viewer
python3 -m http.server 8123
# open http://localhost:8123
```

Handy URLs: `?view=lab` (camera on the bench), `?view=hand` (camera on the
hand), `?t=4.5` (freeze the animation at any second — 4.5 = aspirating,
8.3 = dispensing).

## Put it on your landing page

**Option A — `<model-viewer>` + the animated GLB (simplest, one tag).**
`<model-viewer>` decodes Draco and plays glTF animations out of the box:

```html
<script type="module"
  src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@google/model-viewer@3.5.0/dist/model-viewer.min.js"></script>

<model-viewer src="«HIGH_ENTROPY».min.glb"
  autoplay animation-name="pipetting"
  camera-controls shadow-intensity="1" exposure="1.1"
  style="width:100%;height:560px"></model-viewer>
```

The same file drops into Blender, Unity, Spline, Framer, Webflow, etc. —
the animation is plain glTF keyframes, no custom runtime needed.

**Option B — live three.js embed (full control).**
Copy `robot.js` + `anim.js` into your site and use the snippet in
`embed.html`. You keep parametric control (`buildRobot({ elbow_offset: 90 })`)
and can re-bake the choreography (edit the waypoints in
`anim.js → buildPipettingClip`).

**Regenerating the GLBs** after parameter changes:

```bash
node bake_glb.mjs
npx @gltf-transform/cli prune robios-pipetting.glb «PATH» --keep-attributes false
npx @gltf-transform/cli optimize «PATH» robios-pipetting.min.glb --compress draco
```

(or click **⬇ animated .glb** in the viewer).

## How the animation is wired

- Every joint is a named pivot `Group` (`J1`…`J7`, `index_MCP_flex`,
  `thumb_IP`, …). The clip drives `J1/J2/J4/J6` with quaternion tracks.
- Liquids are named nodes driven by scale/position tracks:
  `pipette_liquid` (rises in the tip), `beaker_liquid` (level drops),
  `well_fill_0/1` (wells fill), `droplet` (forms, falls, vanishes),
  `pipette_plunger` (press/release).
- Because all tracks bind by node name, the clip survives GLB export and
  plays in any glTF runtime.
- `solveArmIK(p, x, y, z)` gives closed-form tip placement (tip pointing
  down), accounting for the 70 mm elbow offset and the pipette grip offset —
  use it to retarget the choreography to your own bench layout.

## Key parameters (`defaultParams` in robot.js)

| Parameter | Default | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `brand_text` | `'ROBIOS'` | engraved name (white, embossed) |
| `color_shell` | `0x16181c` | body color (black) |
| `link1_len` / `link2_len` | 320 / 280 | upper arm / forearm |
| `elbow_offset` | 70 | lateral elbow offset with S-curve hull |
| `finger_scales` | .95/1/.95/.8 | per-finger scale |
| `seam_groove_width` / `pin_clearance` | 1.5 / 0.4 | machining details |

Lab bench layout (beaker/plate positions, well targets, pipette grip) lives in
`LAB` at the top of `anim.js`.

All done and verified — refresh **http://localhost:8123** to watch it run.

## What was built

**The pipetting animation (13 s seamless loop, fully implemented):** the black ROBIOS arm starts upright gripping a micropipette in its fist (fingers curled around the barrel, plunger sitting by the thumb, tip leading beyond the fingertips), folds down, hovers over a glass beaker, dips the tip below the liquid surface and aspirates — the plunger releases, cyan liquid visibly rises into the tip, and the beaker level drops — then swings across the bench and dispenses into **two wells** of a 24-well plate: droplets form at the tip, fall, and the wells fill, then it returns home and loops. Every arm pose is solved with closed-form IK (the smoke test verifies tip placement at **0.00 mm error** through the real scene graph), then baked into a standard glTF `AnimationClip` named `pipetting` sampled at 20 fps.

**Engineering under it:** the robot was refactored from static geometry into a true kinematic hierarchy — all 28 joints are now named pivot groups (`J1`…`J7`, `index_MCP_flex`, `thumb_IP`, …), which is what makes both the animation and the export work.

**Black + ROBIOS branding:** body shells are now black with steel-grey pins; "ROBIOS" is embossed in white in four places — wrapped around the pedestal front *and* back, reading up the upper arm, up the forearm, and on the dorsal palm. The text conforms to the tapered link surfaces and is plain geometry, so it survives every export. Change it via the `brand_text` field in the GUI or `defaultParams`.

## Portable downloads (animation travels with the model)

| File | Use |
|---|---|
| `robios-pipetting.min.glb` (2.9 MB) | **The one for your landing pages** — robot + beaker + plate + liquids + the animation in one Draco GLB. One `<model-viewer autoplay animation-name="pipetting">` tag plays it (snippet in README). I verified the optimized file reloads and plays through a stock glTF loader. Also drops into «HIGH_ENTROPY». |
| `robios-arm.min.glb` (178 KB) | Static black ROBIOS arm, open hand, no props. |
| `robot.js` + `anim.js` | Live three.js kit — full parametric control and re-targetable choreography (bench layout lives in `LAB` at the top of `anim.js`). `embed.html` is the copy-paste example. |

The viewer's buttons (bottom right) export all of these on demand: **animated .glb**, **static .glb**, **robot.js**, **anim.js**.

Useful while exploring: `?view=lab` frames the bench, `?t=4.5` freezes mid-aspirate, `?t=8.3` mid-dispense. One honest caveat: on loop restart the well fills and beaker level reset instantly — standard for looping hero animations, but tell me if you'd rather have a fade-out segment before the loop point.
